Coria vs Gurule
Coria KO RD 2

Kind of random matchmaking here; Gurule is fighting for his job and Coria is coming off of a very impressive debut against one of the higher ranked guys outside the top 15. This is basically just a fury fc title fight in the prelims. The matchmaking is weird to me because this is a mismatch, I expected Coria to get a bigger fight; this is a step down, as bad as Costa looked last fight he’s still way better than Gurule. I never liked Gurule prior to dwcs and he has continued to be unimpressive in the ufc. He had a flashy record coming in but it was all a facade; he is a nothing burger who wins all his fights by going through the motions. He doesn’t excel anywhere, doesn’t have any real finishing ability, doesn’t wrestle enough, and he has bad defense. Gurule also came to the ufc at 31, so he was already a finished product. Losing to Ode was bad but at least he won the first round, the Aguilar loss was absolutely terrible though. I think Aguilar is meme finish or bust yet Gurule lost a decision to him. Gurule was winning the minutes but Aguilar was able to steal rounds because he was landing all the power shots; nothing Gurule landed had any effect and he was getting hit easily. Aguilar has surpassed my expectations but that fight showed me Gurule likely is not going to win any fights in the ufc; he has decent skills but he just isn’t good enough in any one category, and lacks the intangibles like power/athleticism/etc to make up for it. Coria on the other hand is very solid in every category, and mixes everything into his game. He can strike or grapple, is a good finisher (despite his patient composure), has very sharp defense, and has a ton of experience despite his age; he has been competing at the highest regional level since his debut, and he has been a prospect since the start of the 2020s. He’s had some bad performances but recently has looked very good. He has shown a full martial arts skillset, has been finishing everyone, and just had a dominant ufc debut where he won every round and got the finish. Coria isn’t perfect of course, but I think he is better than Gurule anywhere the fight goes. To beat Coria, Gurule would either need to come in with a wrestling heavy gameplan (which he has never done), or he would need to ko/outstrike Coria. Outstriking him is going to be extremely difficult imo, as Coria can easily outpace him, has way sharper defense, significantly more power, and just all around better technique compared to Gurule. Coria generally has extremely sharp defense but when he does slip up, he usually gets hit big. He isn’t chinny at all but the rare times he does let his guard down, he is usually eating a big shot flush on the chin. That’s really my only concern on the feet here but again, Gurule has legitimately no power at all so I think Coria will be safe. He did get stunned pretty good against Ocon, Moran, and Trujillo though, so this is a recurring issue. I really don’t see Coria having many issues handing business here though. I think Gurule is very bad and doesn’t really offer much of anything to Coria. He can easily outstrike him and land bigger shots, and Coria is a better grappler too if he wants to go that route. I think Coria should find another finish here; I lean ko because he likes to strike but a submission wouldn’t shock me either. Coria has been wrestling a lot more recently and his top control has looked great.

Edwards vs Cornolle II
Edwards SUB RD 2

Normally I hate when the ufc does these random WMMA rematches, but I actually think this one makes sense. Edwards has improved a solid amount since then, and the first fight was extremely controversial; it was a very strange fight but I thought Edwards clearly won with control, yet the French judges disagreed. Edwards was terrified to strike/do danage, which is why she “lost”, but she also got easy takedowns with little to no effort. Edwards was also in awful form when that fight took place, I think she is still very flawed but she has shown a lot of improvements since moving to Vegas full time. She’s been fighting cans but she’s been handling them with ease and even took a round off of Perez. Cornolle is a fun fighter but I’ve always thought she is pretty mid, and she’s already hit her ceiling in the ufc; she really hasn’t improved at all. The first fight was weird because Edwards gassed after 7 minutes, wanted nothing to do with the striking, and let Cornolle hit reversals on her in every round. Since then, Edwards grappling has looked much better (despite the goofy Perez fight), her cardio has looked better, and she’s gained more striking confidence than ever. I still don’t think she’s a great striker, but she is not afraid to get into a scrap now. Cornolle hasn’t changed anything since their last fight, so Edwards should know what to do here. She was able to easily win with the grappling, and now I think she has the confidence to stand with Cornolle if need be. Cornolle’s grappling has honestly looked worse and worse every fight, and Edwards was easily taking her down on each attempt. I expect her to wrestle again, but this time she will be more active. I could see her getting reversed again since her control honestly is not the best, but if that happens I expect Edwards to not panic like last time. She freaked out and let Cornolle swing the momentum because she would immediately turtle up and start defending. Edwards is still really hittable but I think she can deal with the striking of Cornolle this time. Cornolle is physical and hits hard but she has no defense and is insanely clunky. In the first fight Cornolle only won the striking because Edwards was legit terrified of her; all she did was play defense and she would shoot the second she had an opening. With her newfound aggression, I think she can bully Cornolle and then mix in takedowns if she isn’t liking how it’s going. Cornolle does have the striking background but she is just a bully to me; I think Edwards can easily land on her and maybe even drop her. I think Edwards is going to hunt for a finish this time, since she knows she got screwed over by the judges. I think sub is more likely but I could totally see a ko too, Edwards hits hard and throws a lot of spinning attacks that Cornolle could run into since she doesn’t move her head well. Even if it does go the distance, I think Edwards is the better minute winner. She will be better prepared for the striking this time and already had easy success with grappling. The Texas judges are historically very bad, but Edwards does have “home court” advantage here since there are going to be a lot of Hispanics in the crowd. I also think UFC put this one together as a getback for Edwards, they definitely didn’t book the rematch to promote the 36 year old who can’t grapple.

Brahimaj vs Soriano
Brahimaj SUB RD 1

Prior to tape I was expecting Ramiz to be a lock. I still think he should win, but the entire public and the betting squares (Clint played 4u on Ramiz, among others) also being on Ramiz makes me a little uneasy. Again, I think Ramiz should win this fight but I also do think he’s being a little overrated here; both of these guys are not very good imo. Ramiz is definitely improving (especially in the mental aspect) but he still lost r1 to Vanderford clearly & was getting touched up by Gall, so the flaws are still there. I never liked Puna at 185 and he has continued to unimpress me at 170. The Medic KO was pretty insane but it was also a 30 second bonk; if that got extended I still think Medic would’ve beaten him. His other two wins have been trash and they were against opponents who just didn’t show up to win; both guys just kept pulling subs and allowing Puna to top time them. Puna never showed wrestling dominance at 185 but imo he hasn’t really done anything new here either; he’s just been facing guys who willingly concede bottom position and have no ground game. I worry a little bit here because Ramiz has never been a good wrestler and historically falls apart if he can’t get ahold of you in r1. He did show good heart and vastly improved striking against Vanderford, but he also conceded takedowns with no effort and easily got outhustled on the mat in r1. I think Vanderford is a significantly better wrestler (and honestly, fighter) than Puna, but even on this win streak Ramiz hasn’t shown any improvements in his offensive wrestling. I did like to see his get ups, elbows, and hustle once he hurt Vanderford, but he was also in panic mode not setting things up. Ramiz can be outwrestled but even in his losses he has done a decent job of getting to his feet. Puna has shown better top control at 170 but I’m really not convinced that he is a good grappler. He’s never gotten a submission and constantly puts himself in bad spots on the mat too. Sadly he will be able to get easy tds here but I think there is a very real possibility he puts his neck right into that front choke/guillotine. Puna has a negative fight IQ and we saw him put his neck into Veretennikov’s lazy guillotine on just about every TD attempt in that fight. That fight as a whole was a terrible look to me. Puna won clearly but he didn’t really do anything with his top control, showed no urgency whatsoever, weirdly gassed hard, and almost got subbed a few times. R2 was especially bad; Veretennikov stood up, got the bodylock, and then Puna literally let him take his back and almost sub him. Even the announcers pointed out how little resistance he showed to the attack. He got lucky that Veretennikov can’t grapple and has no iq, but if he lets Ramiz get to that position the fight is going to be over shortly after. I don’t think his offensive wrestling is very good, but Ramiz can easily get tds of his own here. Puna’s wrestling has always been super overrated imo, and he shows almost no resistance to tds when attempted on him. The striking is a little scary here. Ramiz seems to be improving his offense, but defensively he is still a nightmare. His footwork is plodding and he doesn’t move his head well at all; he got stunned a few times in the Gall fight. Luckily for him Puna is also a low iq, plodding, brawler but he does have nukes in his hands. Ramiz can easily hurt him on the feet and win minutes but there is also a very real chance that Puna lands that overhand and nukes him. The one thing I’ve liked to see from Ramiz’s striking is that he has more confidence. His technique has never been bad but he has always given up when forced to strike for longer than a few minutes and reacted very poorly to being hit. On this win streak, he has been walking through strikes and hurt 2/3 of his opponents. Puna has power but he is extremely clunky and doesn’t have good defense either, he’s been dropped by some weak shots in the past. Ramiz has also switched camps to VFS and is clearly on steroids now, so I think at 33 he is actually improving, where Puna is still the same guy imo. I really don’t like the public confidence in Ramiz, but I do think he’s a better and less reckless fighter than Puna. Puna is still a fraud imo despite being 3-0 at 170, Ramiz is at least showing some evolution as a fighter. One thing I am confident in is that this fight is not going past 1.5 rounds.

Lebosnoyani vs Rowe
Lebosnoyani DEC

I really like JPL as a prospect but I don’t like this fight for him. I think he should be the favorite but this is a much harder fight than Vanderford would’ve been; Rowe has his flaws but he is massive for 170 and very dangerous at all times in the fight. This fight really is just an iq check for JPL; if he shoots takedowns he can easily outgrapple Rowe, but if he chooses to strike this then it becomes a much harder fight. Rowe is super beatable on the feet but I don’t like JPL’s defense nor his willingness to brawl; I like it in certain matchups but not against a guy with an 8 inch reach advantage and tons of power. JPL is a pretty solid striker but he is quite hittable. He’s extremely tough but both of his losses are by KO, and his defense doesn’t look that improved from prior fights imo. He’s actually got quite a good chin but I do worry about him getting too aggressive and getting bonked. Despite this flaw, I wouldn’t be shocked at all if he can win the striking. Pre-tape I was expecting a massive advantage for Rowe, but he honestly is a pretty ****** striker; he just gets carried by his reach and power. Rowe doesn’t even use his reach, he chooses to brawl up close with you and gets hit a lot. It’s tricky to deal with but he has many flaws that can be exposed. For one, he’s just as hittable as JPL and because he’s so tall, he has that stiff defense we often see from bigger guys. Rowe is also extremely susceptible to leg kicks, which JPL will mix in a lot. Rowe is very hard to finish but his chin is weirdly awful; he’s been dropped 4 times through ufc/dwcs and almost got KO'ed by Niko Price a few years back as well. JPL doesn’t have insane ko power but he is very aggressive and will throw big strikes like head kicks so I could see him dropping Rowe. JPL also has experience fighting lanky strikers in Kuiks/Congdon; obviously both are bad and don’t have the freakishly long reach of Rowe, but he at least has shown he can deal with taller guys. If JPL grapples he will dominate this fight and honestly maybe even find a finish, but he loves to strike. He moved camps full time to Syndicate and the UFC wants to push him, so you’d assume he knows he can easily win minutes there. I think his BJJ is top notch and honestly the best part of his game, but all he wants to do on tape is have a 15 minute kickboxing match. Rowe won’t shoot on him so it’s all up to JPL. I could see JPL knocking him off balance/dropping him with a leg kick, and Rowe is very clumsy in general (see the end of r2 with Loosa) so he could just fall to his back, which are avenues for JPL to get his grappling going. His control honestly is pretty bad, and he will give up positions to go for subs though. Rowe has no urgency to get up so JPL should be able to control him, but he also loves to go for triangles and leg attacks rather than traditional RNC’s or arm triangles. Rowe is actually a pretty good grappler himself and has never been subbed, but I do feel like JPL can catch him in something. Rowe is tricky because he always weasels his way to wins. but at the end of the day he’s losing most of these fights until he isn’t, and to poor competition as well. He is also taking this on short notice and has godawful fight iq as it is, so I am expecting him to make some stupid ass mistakes at some point. I’m not confident because JPL is likely going to stand with him for all 15, but I do think he is the better, much more reliable fighter here. He has an extremely easy path to victory if he just listens to his coaches, but I do think he can win the striking too. Rowe knocking him out for being too ****y wouldn’t shock me, but I have to go with the better guy here.

Del Valle vs Leavitt
Del Valle KO RD 2

I have no idea what to make of this fight. I have been fading Del Valle since DWCS with no avail, and Leavitt is someone I always pick against as well. I’ve never picked either of these guys to win a fight at the ufc level, and I hold a 2-4 pick record with both. I really think Del Valle is a fraud but at the same time I’m not sure this is the spot. I always underrate Leavitt’s skillset but I’ve never liked his striking or his wrestling; the former he is still yet to be tested on. Del Valle is very hard for me to rate because his Fury fights are just one bad look after another and he’s shown the same flaws in the UFC, yet he keeps winning fights. It’s still hard to get a read on him though as his opponents have been two jobbers and then a guy who quit literally took a dive. As low as I rate Leavitt, he is a step up in competition (Dulgarian doesn’t count). On the feet I think Del Valle should be fine, but I really worry what happens if Leavitt gets on top; for all his flaws, he is a very crafty grappler. His wrestling is not good imo, but Del Valle has this awful habit of pulling guillotine on every td attempt that comes his way. He did it in just about every Fury fight and was still doing it on DWCS. He’s been better about it in the UFC but again, he hasn’t really faced a good td attempt yet. Leavitt has been subbed before but I am not sold on Del Valle’s guillotine being any good; he could tap him with it but I think it’s unlikely. If he does this and ends up on the mat, he honestly might be cooked. We have seen improvements in his game but you go back to those Fury fights and he legit looks clueless off of his back. Leavitt isn’t some monster with top control but if you’re just going to sit there he will look for submissions of his own or throw some gnp. I do think Del Valle can get up but I worry about him doing something stupid and paying the price. Despite coming down to 145 I don’t think Leavitt is going to have much of a size/strength advantage, he isn’t the most physical guy to begin with and I think his lack of success in the ufc has more to do with his skillset than the weight class. This is also a bad matchup for him in regard to the new weight class, as Del Valle is extremely physical and very clearly on a lot of steroids. I kind of think he can bully his way out/into positions despite Leavitt being the more technical guy. He can also easily score tds of his own if he wants; I’m not sure it’s the best idea but Leavitt can’t defend to save his life and we’ve seen him get top timed on a few occasions. Literally all of his losses come from him getting bullied on the mat and he’s been subbed before; he has flair but I don’t think Leavitt is some exceptional grappler. If Del Valle chooses to keep this standing, I think he should cook Leavitt fairly easily. Leavitt has only shown leg kicks at range and some knees from the clinch, and he’s exclusively fought grapplers in the ufc, so he’s never really faced any striking adversity before. He doesn’t strike me as someone that likes to get hit very much and he hasn’t fought anyone like Del Valle, who is going to force a car crash for 15 minutes. Much like with his grappling, Del Valle’s striking looked terrible in Fury. I still don’t think it’s that good but on dwcs he forced the most absurd pace ever, so at least he has some confidence now; the roids he’s on also allow him to push this crazy pace, as he was gassing after 5 minutes in every regional fight. I don’t think he hits super hard, but I can see that aggressive pace giving Leavitt absolute hell on the feet; like I said, he’s never really been hit before, and in a new weight class I think we could see him run into a wall. I still think Del Valle may be fraudulent, so I’m not very confident in this one but I also think this is a much different style for Leavitt than pure grapplers with durability issues.

Miller vs Judice
Judice KO RD 1

Really fun fight to kick off the card; it looks super low level on paper but I am a huge Judice fan. This is a squash match but I like it for her as it gets her more experience; I think she has a super high ceiling but she got to the ufc with no experience, so it’s good to get more fights at the ufc level. For all her flaws, Miller is a very good grappler. I think she’s awful but Judice has only been matched up with strikers thus far so it’s a change of pace. That being said, I don’t think Miller will have much success here. Again, Judice has not fought any grapplers but the few times she’s faced takedowns she has done all the right things; she’s also scored a few tds of her own so I think she is a capable grappler. Judice has a long and wiry frame that makes her difficult to get ahold of anyways, and she is a good athlete which also helps out. Miller’s wrestling is pretty much nonexistent though, and she’s only taken down girls with white belt level grappling. She can’t wrestle in the open and relies on you either shooting on her (won’t happen here) or clinch tds, which I think will be difficult because of Judice’s frame. Miller will also have to get past the striking of Judice, which is going to be a tall task; she is only one fight removed from an embarrassing KO 1 loss to Santos and didn’t show us anything new in that department in her comeback fight. Judice is a very legit striker imo. She lacks strength/physicality for the weight class but she hits decently hard, has a diverse striking game, and has some of the most absurd pacing I’ve seen, regardless of gender. She fights very educated despite her inexperience too, she never panics and always has good composure. You can make an argument that she’s undefeated and she went toe to toe with much better strikers than Miller. Miller’s striking is amateur level imo, she just flails her arms around wildly with no process or defense. Judice won’t be deterred by her crazy style or wild pace because she can easily crack her on the feet and loves a high paced fight herself. She will also rip leg kicks and body shots, which Miller has never had to deal with nor will she have any answer on how to defend. On the feet I really don’t see any path to victory for Miller outside of some perfectly timed knockout. I think the ferocious striking pace of Judice will make it hard for Miller to close the distance, and I think she is really going to hate getting hit by Judice. If Judice is somehow struggling early, Miller has bad cardio and there is no way she will be able to hold up against the pace Judice sets. I think this will be a pretty wild fight but I expect Judice to win this one quick and easily.

posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.content:
Predictions: 11 of 14 Winners, 4 Perfect, 740 Points
|
Tied for 41st with 4 others

Spivac vs Delija
Delija KO RD 1

An actual good HW scrap for once. There’s probably a high chance this turns into a boring clinch fest but both of these guys are flakes so I think a finish is live for both. I’m tempted to go with Spivac, but I think Delija is a difficult matchup for him. If Delija fights smart I think this looks a lot like the Tybura fight, but if he tries to force the clinch like he does in most of his fights, he will get tossed here. Delija’s tdd and defensive grappling is fine, but he has not looked good when put on his back. Out in the open he will be able to defend Spivac’s tds no problem, but in the clinch Spivac will send him flying. Spivac can’t wrestle out in the open at all, but he is a very good judoka. One thing I’ve never liked about Delija is that he will constantly press you against the cage or even engage in the wrestling first, despite being ahead on the feet. He did it in both his ufc fights thus far, and did it in almost every PFL fight. This worries me a lot not just because it plays right into Spivac’s game, but Spivac is just stronger in the clinch in general. I really don’t know why Delija does it, because he doesn’t spend a ton of time there nor does he do much clinch striking either. If he doesn’t force the clinch, I don’t think Spivac gets much grappling going at all. Spivac only has judo, he can’t wrestle in the open and when he does, his attempts are poor. He has always struggled against guys who are faster than him/move alot, which Delija does. Delija is basically just a lhw that doesn’t cut, and as a result he is so much quicker than everyone in the division. Spivac lost some weight but he is super slow and not a good athlete at all. His striking honestly isn’t bad at all but he doesn’t hit very hard and has the worst body language ever. He shells up the second you start to hit him and looks like he wants to cry when he gets hit clean. His last fight against Waldo was so bizarre; he was advancing the entire time but wasn’t shooting any takedowns at all. He wouldn’t do anything with the pressure and was getting easily picked apart on the outside from Waldo. I think Spivac can land on Delija but I don’t think he has the power to hurt him too badly here. Delija’s hand speed is nasty and Spivac is going to hate getting hit by him. Prior to the eye poke against Waldo, Delija was landing big on him and looked pretty sharp; I don’t think Spivac can handle those initial flurries. As long as Delija doesn’t initiate the clinch, I think Spivac is going to strike with him and get overwhelmed. Spivac can hang with Delija but he is at major speed/confidence disadvantage and doesn’t have good durability either. Delija has had some bizarre showings and been very fragile at times, but I would be really shocked if Spivac is able to hurt him standing; it would have to be gnp or a sub. It’s an intangible but I also just don’t like Spivac’s mentality; he seems a bit checked out currently. He ducked Shamil on fight week and if you go to his ig it’s a lot of him teaching grappling or doing non fight related things. Not super confident since it’s HW and anyone can fall apart, but I think Delija’s striking makes this a quick night; all his IQ/grappling flaws won’t matter if Spivac gets clipped early.

Smith vs Harrell
Smith KO RD 2

I like this fight a lot more than the original booking. I don’t think it’s as tough of a fight for Jacobe but he is still getting a good opponent. Harrell has been grinding his ass off since his fight with JDM got cancelled. I really like his style and think he will do well in the ufc, but this is not a good matchup for him. Like his name implies, Harrell is a muscle hamster. He is big for the division weight wise, but he has a small frame; he’s built like Michel Prazeres. His whole game is based around being a grinder. He looks to close the distance and swing hard while walking you down and mixing in tds. He doesn’t get tired at all and will force a hectic pace in every fight. The caveat of this style is that he is often losing the early rounds. He is incredibly tough but because he’s so small he often has to take one to give one. He’s not a bad striker at all but he takes a lot of hits in the process, especially early. He hasn’t fought anyone who’s necessarily a hard hitter though, which I think is a problem. He’s definitely more comfortable and probably a better striker than Jacobe, but Jacobe has one punch ko power. He’s still not a great striker but his punching mechanics are great, and everything he throws has natural, explosive power behind it. I can see Harrell’s constant, in-your-face pressure giving Jacobe a lot of trouble but at the same time he doesn’t have a ton of power himself, so Jacobe could swing things with one shot. I am a little worried what will happen if Josiah puts him on the back foot though, as Jacobe’s cardio isn’t the best; we’ve seen this style beat guys it’s not supposed to many times before. Harrell is a very solid wrestler/grappler himself. Obviously not as good as Jacobe but he is difficult to hold down, has really solid scrambling ability, and can wrestle his ass off for 15 minutes. I think Jacobe will be able to outwrestle Harrell though, I think his wrestling is elite. His entries are perfect and he has translated his wrestling skills very well to MMA. He can wrestle out in the open which is rare, and has great cage wrestling too. This is topped off with elite athleticism, which allows him to get that upper hand in many positions. Harrell is a really solid wrestler/grappler, but he has been put on his back on a few occasions and held there. I think he will be able to take Harrell down pretty easily. Holding him down may be a little difficult but at the same time, Jacobe is a much higher level wrestler than anyone Harrell has fought before. Jacobe didn’t pace himself very well against Price but I think that was just him being a little green. It does worry me in this matchup because Harrell’s entire game is breaking you down with his pace, but I think Jacobe will fight smarter. If you watch his Fury fights that went to DEC, he did slow but he was being much more efficient with his top control and didn’t care if it was boring. I don’t think he will lay n pray Harrell, but I do think he can be more economical with his grappling. I was also very impressed with the sub he hit on Price. It not only shows evolution to his game, but the back take he hit was extremely slick; showing how far his athleticism can take him. I really like Harrell as a fighter but I think this is a tough ask on short notice. I know Jacobe got a lot of hate for the Price fight, but I still think he is a really talented fighter. He can probably KO Harrell I think, the power and size difference will be an issue here.

Reese vs Pereira
Pereira KO RD 1

Such a goofy fight, this is fantastic matchmaking. This is also a nightmare to pick since both guys are bad and Michel is in one of the worst sumps I’ve ever seen. I’m not surprised that his career turned out the way it has, but it is shocking how little effort he’s shown since losing to Fluffy. He always had bad cardio at 170 but in the Fluffy/Abus fights he was done after like 60 seconds. Getting ko’ed by Daukaus was insane but even as low as I rate him, I still think he’s better than Reese. Michel might be completely done but I’m not sure if he’s bad enough to the point he loses to Reese. I’ve never thought highly of Reese at all; I am surprised he has a winning record in the ufc, but he’s also fought the worst competition possible and been smoked against the only real guys he’s fought. Even washed I think this is a massive step up in competition. Michel has historically dominated against this level of fighter, and Reese hasn’t shown any improvements to me at all. My main worry here is Michel’s motivation, even if he shows up with 1 minute of cardio I honestly think that could be enough to beat Reese. Getting bonked by Daukaus in a minute is very concerning, but at the same time I think Reese’s finishing upside is so overrated. He has one ko at the ufc level and it was against a completely washed Marquez. He definitely hits hard but he is not a one punch threat against most guys; it’s odd because he looks like his strikes really hurt, but then you see him land flush on McVey/Medina/Dusko and it ends up having next to no effect at all. None of those guys have good chins either, so not being able to even slightly wobble them is a really bad look imo. Reese has some good physical tools with his athleticism and massive frame, but I really think his striking sucks. He throws everything straight down the middle which I do like, but he is a limited boxer with very overrated power and classic tall man’s defense. He is stiff as a board and has been brutally ko’ed in all of his losses. Surprisingly, he hasn’t paid for this bad defense on the feet yet, but I do not think he can take clean strikes from Michel early. He will be a glaringly open target to hit and while fresh, Michel has more power than he’s ever had to deal with before. Michel also loves that front kick to the body, and has hurt multiple people with it. It will be very open due to the height difference, but we’ve also seen Reese hurt with body shots before; McVey nearly finished him a couple times with his clinch knees and Reese was not reacting well at all. Reese’s pace could be a problem since Michel has no cardio and can’t be the nail, but I also think he is in a ton of danger early; he’s just so damn stiff and I don’t trust his durability at all. I think Michel could mix in grappling too if he wants. Reese is “dangerous” from his guard but he’s not really a good grappler, just has long legs to throw up a triangle/armbar. His tdd is trash and he doesn’t have much off of his back as long as you survive the first sub attempts. Michel has an extremely sneaky back take where he gets to the cage and uses a standing rnc attempt as leverage; he subbed Michal with this and used this same move against Khaos/Price/Imdaev. I could totally see him getting Reese with it too, as Reese has never fought someone as dynamic or explosive as Michel (at least while fresh). There is a chance that he’s just completely done but all we need is one round of the old Michel and I think he can smoke Reese early. I’ve seen him going like a maniac in sparring with Strickland so I’m hoping that he’s at least slightly more prepared for a war this time. As bad as his last ko was, Michel is generally a pretty durable guy. I think Reese’s finishing ability is way overrated, and as long as Michel isn’t completely done as a fighter I think he is a level above Reese.

Njokuani vs Leal
Leal KO RD 2

Can’t wait for this fight, lowkey one of the best on the card. Both of these guys are ultra violent strikers and I am expecting a war for however long this lasts. I’ve always liked Chidi and I acknowledge how dangerous he is, but this line makes absolutely no sense to me; Chidi feels mushed. Now, I agree with the consensus that Chidi can absolutely put Leal’s lights out, but Chidi being pickem when he has 3 minutes to win this fight and historically has struggled against durable brawlers seems so wrong to me. On rewatch Leal’s striking defense really is alarmingly bad, but I don’t think he’s a chinlet at all. He does play with fire by keeping his hands down and rolling with everything, but up until last fight that wasn’t an issue. Obv getting one tapped like that is never a great look, but I don’t think his chin is magically going to be gone; he was going to get slept at some point with how he fights. Chidi can definitely do a repeat of that but again, I really disagree with this being lined so close; after about 3 minutes this fight heavily favors Leal. Leal will invite openings for Chidi to bonk him, but this in-your-face brawler style is something Chidi has never done well with. For his entire career Chidi has been an awful nail, and someone who can’t fight through adversity at all. He has improved in the latter stage of his career but almost all of his UFC losses look identical to Bellator Chidi; just falling apart at the first sign of adversity. His pure Muay Thai skills are top notch but Chidi has to be the one controlling the fight or else he struggles hard, which Leal will not do. I also really don’t like Chidi at 170. On paper he is 3-1 at 170 in ufc and one of the biggest WW on the roster, but I still think the weight cut affects him greatly. He can make weight fine (sometimes) but I don’t think he can push himself the same way. His 3 wins have all been very mid performances (even ZDS, where I was very confident Chidi would win) and none of the guys are still in the ufc. McKee and Gooden he just hugged in the clinch for 15 min, and while he did soundly outstrike ZDS, Chidi got hurt very badly in r1, telling me his chin is still glass at 170. His size and power translated well, but he has lost a lot of pop imo. He does not fight aggressively at all at 170, and he is still yet to be hit clean; a grazing overhand from ZDS put him on chicken legs, so I am not sold that he can get into another war. I also dont like this clinch heavy style he’s adopted because that will play directly into Leal’s game. Chidi’s clinch is disgusting but that will also invite Leal to start slugging overhands, which is where he thrives. Leal’s pressure is going to give Chidi a ton of issues because he needs range to get his game off. He’s still very dangerous but when you brawl with Chidi he loses a lot of technical ability and his massive range. He has good clinch knees but his actual boxing is poor in the pocket. Leal does get wild swinging crazy overhands but he hits so hard and never lets up on the pressure; I really don’t think you need to hit hard to hurt Chidi either, he has the Anthony Smith chin where his body draws a visceral reaction any time he gets hit. Leal isn’t going to give Chidi a slow paced striking match at range either, and we have plenty of footage of Chidi’s bad cardio; he was gassy even at 185. Leal can also go for tds here if he wants; the last time he faced someone this size (Sy) Leal aggressively wrestled him in r2/3, and I actually thought he won the fight. His wrestling is nothing special but Chidi’s ground game is next level bad, he is a fish out of water and once tired, Leal could really punish him if he wants. I can totally see Chidi deleting Leal early with a knee or his 1-2 but outside of the first three minutes, I really think this is a complete nightmare matchup for Chidi. He does not have the durability/cardio/heart to get into a dogfight, and I don’t understand how a fight can be pickem when he has a very short window to get the finish. Leal obviously is a wildman but I don’t think he’s magically a chinlet just because his chin finally got cracked. We saw him face a very similar style in Michal, and Michal tanked everything and broke Chidi after literally 2 minutes of fighting.

Osbourne vs Alibi
Alibi DEC

I went really in depth on this one and I still don’t really have a good read. Alibi was very disappointing in his ufc debut but I do think he’s better than what he showed; he’s still got his fair share of flaws and is close to a finished product at 31, but his regional tape was better than I remembered. I’m still not sure what to think of Morales but I think it is worth noting he had prior ufc experience, more pro experience in general, and is on a crazy run currently. Alibi looked bad but he also looked much less composed compared to his other fights; he was throwing a lot of spinning attacks with no setup and was panicking every time Morales crashed into the pocket. His wrestling is usually better than what he showed as well; again I think due to debut jitters. He defended well initially but made some crucial mistakes, and prior to the sub he had gotten hurt with body shots, which again I believe caused him to panic. Alibi does throw a lot of singular kill shots like in his debut, but he set them up much better in regional fights. I also liked the pressure he would set in some fights (Silva, Santos), he walked his opps down throwing heat the entire time. His boxing technique is honestly kind of crisp, I wish he would go to it more. Alibi has a sharp jab and 1-2, and I like his body punches as well. I don’t think Alibi likes getting hit very much but when he focuses more on landing good strikes over dynamic kill shots, I think he is actually a pretty serviceable striker. Ode is a tricky guy to deal with because he’s the size of a FW and has one punch ko power, but his striking is honestly awful. Ode gets carried by his athleticism and massive frame, his actual striking process is nonexistent. Ode can be dynamic and his reach allows him to throw strikes from weird positions, but he doesn’t really ever put combos together and outside of round 1 he just starts throwing singular hooks/straights; even other announcers noticed this in the Lazy/Erceg fights. He nearly killed both in r1 and then just completely lost focus and let them back into the fights. It’s partially a skill/mentality thing but Ode’s fight IQ is among the worst in the ufc. He doesn’t fight with a gameplan or process, and constantly makes bad decisions. I backed him to beat Lazy Bum and he had every opportunity in the world to get that win, but he kept forcing grappling when he didn’t need to. Ode also has bad cardio because of his weight cut, and as a result he slows down massively after r1. Ode is honestly skilled everywhere but there’s just zero semblance of a gameplan; he has close to no decision equity, has no bottom game, and is fragile as well. What worries me here is that he is physical enough to cause a lot of issues for Alibi; I don’t think he’s the strongest guy and his offensive tds have looked mid at best. As is the case in all Ode fights though, just don’t get ko’ed in r1 and this becomes extremely winnable. Ode’s lack of process will invite Alibi to get ahead on strikes and because of how hittable Ode is, I can actually see one of his wild spinning attacks getting through. Ode’s reach is scary but he also doesn’t fight behind it very well, so if Alibi can get some confidence on the feet I think he can actually win the striking at a decently high clip. Ode’s tdd is actually a lot better than I remembered, as well as his grappling. However, much like with the striking, he has no process there either. He has some flashy subs but once on his back, has some of the worst bottom game in the entire ufc. He will just lay there doing absolutely nothing, and make zero attempts to get to his feet. Alibi’s wrestling and grappling did not look good last fight but I do think Morales is a very high level grappler so I’ll cut him some slack. Ode can throw some subs up but he hasn’t gotten one in almost 7 years, so I think Alibi will be fine. Alibi’s wrestling does not seem good to me at all, but if he tries enough I think he can get Ode down. Once on top, I think Alibi can easily ride out top control and maybe even find a sub. I’m not very confident in this fight at all, I do like Alibi but I’m not sure if he’s ufc level. Ode has always been an auto fade for me though; he is naturally gifted but he’s also one of the dumbest fighters on the roster and has fumbled multiple fights before. Outside of r1 I think Alibi can grind him out, and maybe find a finish; I’m on the fence for Alibi by finish or decision. If Ode wins I feel like it has to be an early KO.

posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.content:
Predictions: 11 of 14 Winners, 4 Perfect, 740 Points
|
Tied for 41st with 4 others

Strickland vs Hernandez
Strickland KO RD 3

Banger main event. Strickland fights aren’t known for being exciting but Fluffy is always a good watch. I think he will bring a fight out of Strickland anyways given his style. I was kind of nervous backing Strickland pre-tape because he seems like a square pick, but after tape I kind of feel like he could win this one. I hate going against guys with momentum like Fluffy, especially when I’ve been wrong on them before, but I really am not sold on him being an elite MW. When he gets his game going it’s awesome to see, and he is a very difficult guy to beat but at the same time the flaws in his game are very clear. A lot of people compare him to Merab and I do get that, but he is also missing the durability and athleticism of Merab. Fluffy’s cardio is next level but he is built like an average joe and hardly cuts weight. His game works because he’s impossible to get off of you but he is very sloppy, gets hit a ton, is tough but doesn’t react well to being hit clean, and he puts himself in a lot of bad spots. His fights are dominant when he starts to slow his opponents down but prior to that they’re honestly really ugly. He gets cracked in almost every r1 but I’ve also seen him taken down very easily, reversed a ton, and put in multiple bad spots on the ground. I don’t want to discredit him because he can’t control who he fights, but I think a lot of his matchups thus far have been extremely favorable; you look at his wins and it’s either complete gas bags, guys who aren’t in the ufc anymore, or guys he’s already beaten prior (Allen). Strickland does not get tired and while a boring fighter, he can easily fight at a high pace when he wants to, so he provides an interesting challenge here. I like backing Strickland because he is consistent and you always know what you’re getting with him. He never deviates his style and always has teh same approach. His striking is basic but effective, he never stops throwing, always pressures, and he has good defensive grappling. I worry about him separating on the feet since he doesn’t have a ton of power and likes to go on cruise control, but he has the ability to defend most of the takedowns and won’t get tired so this is a good test for Fluffy. I honestly think Fluffy’s grappling is a little overrated. I don’t mean it like he’s not good at it, but I think his constant pressure and nonstop shooting helps carry him quite a bit. On tape, he takes a lot of really bad/sloppy shots that either whiff, or put him on bottom. He’s able to bail himself out because he knows he’s a better grappler, but he’s very lackadaisical and I do question his effectiveness against someone who's hard to wrestle. I think he can take Strickland down but nobody has been able to hold him down for longer than a few seconds. There was Abus taking his back at the end of r1 which was not a great look, but Abus is also gigantic and while fresh a very dynamic fighter. If Fluffy can get him in that same position it could get really interesting but at the same time he really is not as dominant of a control guy as he seems. While his opponents are fresh, they are able to get up with surprisingly not much effort at all. Dolidze would get taken down for 5-10 seconds and then would immediately be up, the corpse of Michel was getting up multiple times in each round, Kopylov was defending everything while fresh, etc. I just personally feel like it’s going to be very difficult for Fluffy to get his game off; not only because Sean has good defensive grappling, but he’s not going to wilt like Fluffy’s prior opponents. On the feet I actually think Fluffy will have some decent success, but mostly due to Strickland’s style. Sean is difficult to back at times because he doesn’t separate the best and has very little power, but he is a very good striker. Fluffy will force a war out of him and while Strickland likes a boring fight, he can absolutely fight in a scrap if he needs to. There’s no flair to Strickland’s game but I think he can outwork Fluffy on the feet here. It will be close because Fluffy will be in his face and Sean doesn’t hit that hard, but in a majority striking fight it’s difficult for me to back him. Fluffy is a very serviceable striker but much like with the tds, I think a lot of his success is predicated on his opponents wilting to his pace. Both guys start their fights off by putting their opponents on the backfoot and pressuring, so it comes down to who can take the first step. This forward pressure of Strikcland is going to be very interesting, as Fluffy hasn’t fought someone who is willingly going to march in front of him. Strickland can be the nail but I’m not sure we can say teh same for Fluffy. He is a tough guy and it’s not like he is gonna gas, but he does not react well when hit clean and is especially weak to the body. He has gotten hurt or stunned big in almost every fight on his winstreak, and his striking gets really ugly in extended exchanges. Fluffy’s survived more powerful, dynamic strikers than Strickland before but again, he hasn’t fought someone who won’t fold to his pace. I know Strickland doesn’t wow anywhere but he is a very difficult guy to beat; his only losses in the last 6 years are the two most recent champs and a robbery to Jared. Like I said in the beginning, I hate going against guys with momentum like Fluffy has, but at the same time I’m really not sold on him as an elite MW. He is a fantastic fighter but the flaws are clearly there; I’m not sure if he can hang with the bigger athletes and more durable guys at the top. If Fluffy wins I actually think it will be a decision; I doubt he can flatten Sean out or get to a dominant enough position. I think it would be a frustrating clinch heavy fight where he just neutralizes Sean’s game. If Strickland wins, I think it will be by KO. I know that’s even more bold than taking Strickland to win, but I have a feeling if he keeps it standing he will get Fluffy out of there. If Strickland is able to negate the wrestling and keep it standing, I feel like he will eventually figure Fluffy out and get a KO; Fluffy is a good striker but far from the scariest guy Strickland has fought. I kind of think it plays out like the Abus fight. Fluffy will be able to suppress him early on but I think Strickland will be able to stay safe and get up. If he’s able to continually get up, I feel like he is slowly going to work at Fluffy and overwhelm him with volume

Neal vs Medic
Medic KO RD 1

Pretty random matchup but this is going to be a banger; two great strikers with a lot of ko power. I really didn’t expect to see Medic drawing top 15 but he did look great in his last fight. This is a big step up in comp but I also think he’s fighting Neal at a very opportune time. I personally think Neal has been kind of overrated for a while, and he is coming off of a brutal KO loss at 35. He’s been ranked forever and given some tough fights to good guys, but if you look at his record he hasn’t really beaten anyone ranked level in a very long time; his best win is either pre prime belal or Luque, and the latter is aging very poorly. Medic does fit the level of comp Neal has been beating, but I think his style could prove very difficult for Neal. Medic has some really bad chin issues and does get dropped a lot but his striking is absolutely nasty. There’s no windup or tell on his strikes, he hits super hard and moves very fast. Uros may be a former 155er but his frame is gigantic (he’s taller than listed, def closer to 6”3) and he fights behind his range well. I think this and the aforementioned speed are going to cause a lot of issues for Neal. Neal has struggled with lanky strikers his entire career (0-5 against that archetype) and Uros has the ability to fight at range with his kicks. Neal needs a brawl to win fights; Uros will throw down with him but most of the time I think he stays educated. He surprisingly doesn’t have a reach advantage but Uros will fight longer since he will be spamming a lot of kicks at distance. Neal is a better boxer but Uros’s hand speed is just as fast (if not faster) and he too possesses one punch KO power. I think Neal’s boxing will be really hard for Uros to get past due to his defensive issues, but at the same time I don’t think Neal has had to fight someone with this fast hand speed in a while. This one probably comes down to who finds the chin first, and in that case you gotta go with Neal. Despite being much less durable than Neal, I think this really just comes down to who can find the chin first. Neal isn’t chinny but we have seen him dropped/hurt before, and he is coming off of the most brutal loss of his career so far. Uros has one punched multiple people as well, so he can just as easily bonk Neal imo. I have a bad feeling this could just be a levels fight and Neal catches him for being too ****y, but we have seen Neal struggle against faster and taller guys throughout his entire career. He is a great boxer but Medic has the size to fight his style of fight, and the speed to catch Neal. I don’t think Uros is anywhere close to Prates, but we saw Neal struggling heavily with his speed there. He was letting Neal close the distance and swing on him, but Prates was able to evade most of his strikes and really punish him for being aggressive. Speaking of the Prates fight, I am curious how Neal shows up after that. Getting ko’ed in that fashion can change you, and Neal has always struggled with the mental aspect of fighting so I am worried that could change his approach. He was looking unusually aggressive against Prates and paid for it, so I wonder if he shows up gunshy and reserved. In that case, Medic is gonna be able to skirt the outside and pick at him with power shots. Neal is the deserved favorite and I really hate how bad Medic’s chin is, but I think this is a matchup he could potentially shine in. Like all Medic fights, I expect this to end very quickly; I really think it is going to come down to who finds the chin first.

Ige vs Costa
Ige KO RD 3

This feels like a classic Ige underdog spot to me. I will say, I have done a very bad job of picking Costa fights, but I’m still not sold on him as a top 15 level guy. He has done very well but a lot of his fights on this current streak have been really ugly. They’ve been insanely exciting but he’s also taken a fair bit of damage in all of them. He’s also been fighting a lot of journeymen or veterans; decent wins but nothing mind blowing. The Charriere win was crazy and definitely props to him for that, but Charriere has been pretty disappointing in the ufc in retrospect. Ige fits the bill of who Melk has been beating recently, but I don’t think this is an easy out. Ige is a very beatable fighter but the only people to have bested him have been top 10 or higher; hardly any beat him without a struggle. Melk has had some wild fights and finishes but none of them have made me a believer that he is a top 15 guy. When he isn’t getting a finish he almost always loses a round, his striking defense is poor, and he gasses too. The Erosa and Ceerod fights were particularly ugly; I get the circumstances weren’t great (short notice + elevation) but he gassed hard in both and honestly went life and death with them (I still think Ceerod beat him). I think Melk is a really crafty striker with especially great kicks, but I don’t like his boxing. He gets hit soooo much and doesn’t have a ton of power in his hands. Again in both the Ceerod/Erosa fights, they were giving him hell in boxing. Ige isn’t as wild as Erosa but he is a very technical boxer with one punch power; I think his finishing ability is a little overrated but when he connects clean on you, it’s usually game over. Ige is also very defensively sound and a great counter striker; which I think is going to go a long way here. Melk will pepper him on the outside with his kicks but once Ige closes the distance I think he will punish Melk bad. Melk doesn’t keep his hands up well and leaves himself open a lot; especially since he is throwing kicks most of the time. His head kick is very dangerous but I think Ige will be prepared. Melk can land more punches than Ige since he has a bad habit of waiting/falling behind, but I think it will be difficult to steal rounds since he lacks power. I also worry about Melk’s durability here, not only is he hittable & been ko’ed twice before, but his style is not designed for the long term. He gets hit a lot in his fights and is on his 5th fight in a calendar year. I also recently saw a training video of him and Brito where they were just having a full on war with no head gear; it’s good to be prepared for a war but between the activity and wars in the gym, I question his durability a little bit. He hasn’t fought a ton of hard hitters on this streak and his last loss was a vicious ko, so it’s not like it’s impossible to finish him. Melk could mix in tds but I’m not sure how successful he will be. Funny enough Ige is 0-7 in fights where he gets taken down, but personally I don’t think Melk’s grappling is on the level of prior opponents. Ige is getting up there in age and you have to assume that his consistency will fail at some point, but I still feel like he is a top ten level guy. I have a bad track record with Melk and he is definitely getting one of the easier top 15 fights, but I am not sold on him personally. I think he is a bad minute winner and I don’t trust him to stay safe for 15 minutes against Ige. He slows down as well so this could get ugly if he isn’t careful.

The one thing about Strickland is that I think he will slow down in rounds 4 and 5. In the DDP fight, he was breathing pretty heavily after like a minute of wrestling and I think Hernandez is gonna force him to do that a lot more. Strickland is not gonna death gas like Dolidze or Pereira but if you slow down against Hernandez even if he doesn't finish you, the visuals will look terrible and you'll probably lose the round. Strickland's path to victory in my opinion is just pressuring hard and shucking off takedowns and breaking from the clinch as fast as possible and just put the pace on Fluffy. Even though Fluffy won't gas, I don't think he handles being pressured well. Strickland has to prevent Fluffy from slowing down the fight and make Fluffy fight at his pace, I just find it hard to believe that Fluffy will allow him to do that and not impose his own will. Both can win, and no one should be -300 but Fluffy is rightfully favored.

Strickland looked absolutely shell shocked in the 2nd DDP fight, and that's the most recent performance of his we have to draw from.

He had no offense, no volume and lacked forward pressure, ended up losing 50-45 on two scorecards. That's not someone I want to back against someone who is going to bring the fight to him just like DDP did.

--

Fluffy isn't god either though, got hurt to the body against Michel, got hurt and outwrestled in some sequences against Brenden Allen.

I do think Sean Strickland is more akin to Roman Dolidze than Brenden Allen though honestly, slow, plodding and very basic offensive techniques.

Also, the Brenden Allen that Sean beat in COVID times is not the Allen that just decimated RDR, he has substantially leveled up.

--

People were calling for Fluffy to fight for the Middleweight title next, now there are several who truly and confidently feel that Sean Strickland will be the one to solve his puzzle; I disagree - Really think Sean loses decisively, even if it is a decision.

I love the fact that Fluffy has been getting rounds in with Oralbai and Satybaldiev, those are two solid training partners to have. Strickland has been stuck in the Xtreme Couture rut for years now.

Lebosnoyani vs. Rowe
Jean-Paul Lebosnoyani, DEC
Lebosnoyani is the pick here. in reccent fights Phil Rowe has shown to be kinda scared to get active, he is too patient and is waiting for a big counter while he is being tit tatted. With a guy like Lebonosyani who chains together either a combo or takedown behind everything he does, I think Rowe will have a hard time getting anything started. I also like Lebonosyani’s crafty ability to get that leg up there like we saw in his DWCS and several of his LFA fights, and he puts together combos with it so beautifully. On the ground, Lebosnoyanni yet again has the advantage with the current version of Phil Rowe he is against. Rowe has been too willing to stay on his back so he can attempt a triangle, but it’s failed him everytime allowing hi sopponent to get the better position with control time. This will happen again if they go the floor. My biggest worry with Lebosnoyanni is that he has to shown to take some damage in his fights, especially taking leg kicks (which crafty phil rowe loves to pump) but I think Lebosnoyanni will out strike Rowe to the dec win.

Strickland vs. Hernandez
Sean Strickland, DEC
I'm pretty torn on this fight to be honest whenever i think about either of their styles and watch tape on them i'm always thinking they've obviously got holes in their game but they have the exact tools to beat the other but they also fight really similarly. They both have insane gas tanks and longevity in fights, they both rely heavily on pressuring their opponents to win, and they also can get beaten when their pace is matched. That being said, i'm gonna side with Fluffy pushing more when things don't start to go his way and i feel like later in the fight he's gonna really grit down for a finish if Strickland does get his gameplan going but that's only if Sean can actually get his gameplan going earlier in the fight. Something i don't understand as well about Sean is the narrative that he's got some sort of brick wall takedown defence when he really doesn't. I mean he has good defensive grappling but it's kinda like JDM he can defend takedowns but he really shines in his scrambling and reversal abilities. However, Hernandez is gonna pressure and just keep shooting everytime Strickland gets or tries to get back up. He's gonna really test Sean's grappling and it'll be good for Strickland because if he can get past this grappling test with flying colours, his chances with a title shot after this one against Khamzat skyrocket. It really is a tough fight to pick but the more i think about it if Strickland can actually handle all the pressure of (Fluffy which i think he can) then he can push his pace on the feet, get his rhythm going and start to actually unload. When Strickland gets someone just as aggressive as him in front of him he really gets after it and i think it'll help his confidence that Anthony Hernandez doesn't have the best defence in the stand up and he gets tagged a lot. I mean the dude was getting cracked by Brendan Allen and Roman Dolidze bruh. That's not a good look going into a fight with a guy that has arguably a better gas tank than yourself and definitely way better boxing and striking in general. I think Strickland gets this win 48-47 but it's gonna be a close decision and i reckon Fluffy's gonna actually drag him into an actual war like he always says he's gonna do. Prolly my pick for FOTN. I do like Hernandez i'm actually starting to hop on the fan train i guess but i'm a De'Sean fan before basically anyone at MW other than Bobby Knuckles. NO BIAS THOUGH i just think he's gonna win. Wouldn't be surprised if Anthony just ragdolls and finishes Strickland on the ground though he's really good in top positions too. I just don't see Sean staying down there for any more than under 2 minutes a round his stand up. I say he gets it done but it'll be a hard fought battle for both of em but Strickland will get himself back in the running and Hernandez will still be a dangerous contender at 185. Both of their stock goes up and they'll prolly both have some crazy viral iron chin moments.

Sean Strickland late TKO or DEC
Sean by jabbin, teepin, and philly shellin

Geoff Neal R2 TKO
Basically who can crack who first, which I would lean Neal. Geoff Neal should win because even though Medic will likely pressure hard early and try to overwhelm Neal, Neal has the experience, game plan (hopefully), and fight IQ to stay composed and survive that initial burst. Medic’s aggressive style leaves many defensive gaps, and against a southpaw boxer like Neal who throws clean counters those openings will be costly.

Melquizael Costa DEC
Costa’s pressure could disrupt the rhythm Ige will want to fight in, and Costa's creativity on the feet makes him a real threat early. If Ige can stick to his technical boxing, control range, and avoid the wild exchanges, his experience and measured approach could carry him through to a decision win. No matter the outcome it will be a fun fight. Well finally see Dan Ige out of the rankings.'

Ante Delija R1 KO
Delija’s explosive power and striking makes him the favorite. Spivac does bring wrestling and grappling to the Octagon, but a lot of people tend to overrate how much his ground game actually changes outcomes. Delija will be able to keep the fight standing and get him out of der in 1.

posts.author: Skweept
posts.content:
Predictions: 9 of 14 Winners, 2 Perfect, 515 Points
|
Tied for 2806th

Neal vs. Medic
Geoff Neal, TKO, R1
I'm pretty much banking that Geoff neals fire has not been lit just yet. both have major power but I think neal has been able to prove that power against way better competition and only get chinned by Carlos prates as far as I remember. Uros medic has kicks and is a lot more jittery and random in his movements which could be a problem for neal because he doesn't do good against tall guys in his career so far. But i don't see why this won't be like the soriahno ko. I don't like how uros does those body or leg kicks and is easily countered most of the time if they are decent. I think uros will be stepping in with a combo or kick and just get met with a right hook or right straight as a counter and uros will fall. Geoff neal will never get a title shot and will probably be fed to someone like michael morales or someone else higher ranked like that that he has no chance against and get flatlined again but this is still a important fight for him and he has to prove that he's still ranked material after all this time by putting out someone who doesn't even deserve this opportunity in the first place.

Ige vs. Costa
Melquizael Costa, DEC
Melquizael costa has been on the rise in 2025, 2 finishes and 2 decision wins. In this matchup costa is gonna have to keep his range with his kicks and long straights. It does worry me how much costa gets hit in his fights especially against julian erosa, erosa obviously is taller than dan ige but does not have nearly the same power as ige. Costa needs to be kicking to all levels to try and set up the head kick and keep him back a bit. I think costa will be able to do that, he has was more ways to win than dan ige who just walks you down and lunges in with a 3,4 combo everytime. But obviously dan always has that punchers chance of putting you out i guess. I think ige will hurt costa with a hook either round 2 or 3 but I think costa will be able to win a decision with kicking to all sides and areas, but also grappling with ige when he needs to, he could clinch ige against the fence if he wanted to and I wouldn't be surprised if costa gets a random sub like he did against fili, not as easily of course because fili literally put himself in there but he could catch ige with something when he's not expecting it or getting up from the ground

Coria vs. Gurule
Alden Coria, TKO, R2
Luis Gurule is just slow to me in almost every aspect of MMA. He's a slow starter, he walks towards you all slow like trying to impose his pressure, he has slow hooks and i haven't seen much combos out of him versus osbourne or aguilar. Alden coria is younger, faster, slick combos, good movement, and pretty decent grappling, I honestly think Alessandro Costa who coria knocked out is better than gurule. Gurule is gonna be trying to walk down coria and throw a couple up top punches to try and get the takedown, but I think corias takedown defense is pretty decent and if he does go down he'll be able to get up reasonably quick. Give me coria by Tko in round 2 with a combo that drops gurule into ground and pound

Judice vs. Miller
Carli Judice, TKO, R2
There's only 1 possible way Carli Judice loses this fight. And that's if Juliana Miller can get ahold of the body lock and trip her and smudge her there for 2/3 rounds. But i do not see that happening. Juliana has terrible striking who relies on the clinch and lazy body locks to win her fights. I think Carli might have a little bit of trouble in the first round with juliana constantly crashing in and trying to ger a hold of her but eventually carli is just gonna either headkick juliana who can't get her hands up quick enough to defend or she's gonna eat a mean combo on the feet and get finished by ground and pound. Carli is personally one of my favorite WMMA fighters, she is one of the few who actually gets finishes and makes fights entertaining and i'll be damned if juliana miller is gonna be the one who ends the run

Coria vs. Gurule
Luis Gurule, TKO, R2
*Que Requeim in D Minor by Mozart*

Enter Coria

CORIA  Why should I play the Texas fool and die On mine own sword? Whiles I see lives, the gashes Do better upon them.

Enter GURULE

GURULE Turn, hellhound, turn!

CORIA Of all men else I have avoided thee. But get thee back. My soul is too much charged With blood of thine already.

GURULE I have no words; My voice is in my sword, thou bloodier villain Than terms can give thee out. Fight. Alarum.

CORIA Thou losest labor. As easy mayst thou the intrenchant air With thy keen sword impress as make me bleed. Let fall thy blade on vulnerable crests; I bear a charmèd life, which must not yield To one of woman born.

GURULE Despair thy charm, And let the angel whom thou still hast served Tell thee Gurule was from his mother’s womb Untimely ripped.

CORIA Accursèd be that tongue that tells me so, For it hath cowed my better part of man! And be these juggling fiends no more believed That palter with us in a double sense, That keep the word of promise to our ear And break it to our hope. I’ll not fight with thee.

GURULE Then yield thee, coward, And live to be the show and gaze o’ th’ time. We’ll have thee, as our rarer monsters are, Painted upon a pole, and underwrit “Here may you see the tyrant.”

CORIA I will not yield To kiss the ground before young Dana White’s feet And to be baited with the rabble’s curse. Though the Meta Apex be come to Dunsinane And thou opposed, being of no woman born, Yet I will try the last. Before my body I throw my warlike shield. Lay on, Gurule, And damned be him that first cries “Hold! Enough!”

They exit fighting. Alarums.

They enter fighting, and Coria is slain. Gurule exits carrying off Coria's body. Retreat and flourish.
Enter Gurle with Coria’s head.

GURULE Hail, King! for so thou art. Behold where stands Th’ usurper’s cursèd head. The time is free. I see thee compassed with thy kingdom’s pearl, That speak my salutation in their minds, Whose voices I desire aloud with mine. Hail, King of UFC! ALL Hail, King of UFC! Flourish.

* Edited at 02.19.2026,  1:07 PM ET *

posts.author: tramenzo
posts.content:
Predictions: 9 of 10 Winners, 6 Perfect, 625 Points
|
Tied for 624th

When it comes to capping whether or not a fighter is at risk of death gassing you have to be really honest with yourself about what you are seeing;

A.) Fighter consistently gasses but still fights functionally while fatigued and will push through it.
B.) Fighter consistently gasses and will look for the door once fatigued (wilts).

Obviously there are other circumstances but generally these are the two types of gassers.

Jacobe Smith & Yadier Del Valle clearly fall into category A.
Jordan Leavitt clearly falls into category B.

Sharp bettors are able to distinguish between what they want to see (because the outcome is best for their wallet) and demonstrated reality (what actually shows up on tape).

* Edited at 02.19.2026,  1:22 PM ET *

I don't think ramiz is a good striker as much as he goes hard in the first round. I think veretennnikov is the better striker compared to ramiz. So the idea is if puna doesn't wrestle offensively he won't look as gassed. Everyone gasses when you wrestle hard or look for the finish early. Vererennikov was going for desperation guillotines instead of prioritizing the defense of the initial takedown shot and landing power shots after the failed takedown because he was also gassed. Themba comfortably cage pushing ramiz for a win is concerning, so I suspect if puna can actually assemble a good game plan here he could win and if it exists round 1 his chances dramatically increase.

posts.author: Lois Waterford
posts.content:
Predictions: 7 of 14 Winners, 2 Perfect, 445 Points
|
Tied for 5111th

Strickland vs. Hernandez
Anthony Hernandez, DEC
I think this comes down to hunger and pace. Sean Strickland has been in big spots recently and came up short in frustrating title fights. That kind of stretch can take something out of a fighter mentally. Hernandez, on the other hand, is surging. He is riding a strong win streak, his cardio is relentless, and his pressure style forces opponents to work every second of every round.  Strickland is still sharp, still durable, and still capable of winning rounds with his jab and defensive shell. But over five rounds, Hernandez’s pace, clinch work, and constant forward pressure should wear him down. I can see Strickland grabbing a round or two early, but the longer this goes, the more it favors Hernandez.

Neal vs. Medic
Uros Medic, TKO, R2
Medic fights like every exchange could end the night. It is chaos, power, and aggression from the opening bell. Geoff Neal is the more polished veteran and probably the more technical striker overall, but timing matters in this sport.  Neal is coming back relatively soon after a brutal knockout loss, and at nearly 36 years old, those kinds of knockouts can linger. Add in a tough weight cut and that becomes even more concerning. If Medic pressures early and forces exchanges, I think he eventually cracks him in the second round for a statement finish.

Ige vs. Costa
Dan Ige, DEC
I like the underdog here. Costa is young, active, and on a strong run, but sometimes activity can work against you. Back to back camps without real recovery can catch up to a fighter.  Ige has faced elite featherweights and never backs down from tough matchups. He has shared the cage with top contenders and knows how to manage high level pressure. Costa has struggled when facing experienced opposition that can mix pace with composure. I think Ige’s veteran savvy and durability help him edge out a competitive decision.

Spivac vs. Delija
Ante Delija, TKO, R1
Delija brings real heavyweight power. When he connects clean, fights end. Sergey Spivac is at his best when he can secure takedowns and control, but recently he has looked slower and a bit inconsistent.  If Spivac cannot get this to the mat early, he is in serious danger. On the feet, Delija hits much harder and throws with bad intentions. I do not see this lasting long if it stays standing.

Smith vs. Harrell
Jacobe Smith, TKO, R2
Both men are undefeated and motivated, but Smith has faced the tougher level of competition so far. He has shown real finishing ability and composure in key moments.  Cardio has been a question mark for him in the past. If he has addressed that, his physicality and pressure could overwhelm Harrell, who is making his UFC debut. Debuts are unpredictable, and that spotlight can be heavy. Smith gets it done in the second.

Reese vs. Pereira
Zach Reese, DEC
Pereira has not looked like himself in recent outings. Reese brings size, reach, and a more complete overall skill set. He can strike at range and also mix in grappling if needed.  With the physical advantages and versatility, Reese does not need to take big risks. I expect him to manage distance, control exchanges, and secure a smart three round decision.

Njokuani vs. Leal
Chidi Njokuani, TKO, R2
Leal loves to chase the knockout and goes all in when he smells blood. Njokuani is more polished and technical, even at 37, and he is a dangerous knockout artist himself. His weakness is clearly on the ground, but I do not see Leal trying to wrestle here. Leal showed defensive lapses in his last fight against an older opponent, and I can see something similar happening again. Njokuani catches him in the second and gets the finish.

Osbourne vs. Idiris
Ode Osbourne, DEC
Osbourne is better than his record suggests. He has fought serious competition at flyweight and has seen different styles at the highest level.  Idiris built his record mostly on the regional scene in Kazakhstan, which does not always translate to UFC level success. His loss in the TUF Finale exposed some gaps. Osbourne’s experience against tougher opposition and higher pace should allow him to edge a close fight.

Coria vs. Gurule
Alden Coria, TKO, R2
Coria is younger, bigger, and carries clear physical advantages. He is riding momentum and looks confident.  Gurule has struggled when stepping up to UFC level competition. If Coria pushes the pace and uses his strength, I see him breaking through in the second round.

Cornolle vs. Edwards
Joselyne Edwards, SUB, R3
This is a rematch of a competitive first fight. Since then, Edwards has looked more aggressive and more physically imposing.  She is the bigger athlete and can use her strength to force grappling exchanges. Cornolle has solid skills, but her weight cuts have appeared to drain her late in fights. If that happens again, Edwards could take over in the third round and lock in a submission to avenge the earlier loss.

Brahimaj vs. Soriano
Punahele Soriano, DEC
Soriano is a powerful striker, especially for welterweight, and when he lands clean he can change a fight instantly. Brahimaj’s biggest strength is his grappling, and Soriano has struggled there before, so that is the obvious concern. Still, I think Soriano fights smart, keeps this at distance, and avoids prolonged scrambles. If he stays disciplined and picks his shots instead of overcommitting, he can win a close decision on the feet.

Lebosnoyani vs. Rowe
Phil Rowe, TKO, R3
On paper, this matchup favors the debuting Lebosnoyani, but experience matters. Rowe has been in there with tough competition and knows how to survive adversity.  He is durable and awkward, and even in losses he makes opponents work hard. If Lebosnoyani cannot get him out of there early, the pressure of a UFC debut combined with extended rounds could lead to fatigue. That is when Rowe becomes dangerous. I like the upset late.

del Valle vs. Leavitt
Jordan Leavitt, DEC
Del Valle is a serious prospect with well rounded skills, especially on the mat. But Leavitt’s recent evolution has been impressive.  He is used to handling strong lightweights, and this bout taking place at featherweight could amplify his strength advantage. Both men enjoy grappling, so this could turn into a fun scramble heavy fight. In tight exchanges on the mat, Leavitt’s unpredictability and experience might be the difference.

Judice vs. Miller
Carli Judice, TKO, R1
Stylistically, this is rough for Miller. Judice is long, explosive, and aggressive from the opening bell. Her speed and reach make it difficult for opponents to close distance safely.  Miller’s grappling is her path to victory, but her striking entries are slow and she has struggled to secure takedowns against strong opposition. If she cannot get this to the ground quickly, she is in serious danger. Judice’s striking could overwhelm her inside the first five minutes.

Hernandez is:  Relentless grappler  Insane pace  Very opportunistic with scrambles  Strickland is:  Elite jab  Strong defensive wrestling  Very hard to drown with pressure  The key question: Can Hernandez hold him down consistently? posts.author: AyyLmaonnaise
posts.content:
Predictions: 11 of 14 Winners, 4 Perfect, 740 Points
|
Tied for 41st with 4 others

I don’t think he’s improved at all at 170; he’s just been fighting easier comp. McKee and Goodn are hardly real wins, and he still got rocked bad against ZDS which tells me his chin isn’t all that great. His cardio hasn’t been tested at all because he’s been allowed to be the hammer; Leal is going to make him the nail. Leal is very hittable but I think he can weather the early storm; I think there is a LOT of recency bias regarding Leal’s chin here. Yes, he has bad defense but he’s never really shown chin issues prior to his last fight. Was it a great look? No, but I think a ko loss was coming with Leal’s style. Chidi can definitely nuke him early but I have such a difficult time backing him when I know he has abojt 3 minutes to win before the swamp forms. I picked Robocop and Michal against him for similar reasons, I think if you are tough enough it’s easy to survive the onslaught. I also think his finishing ability is t nearly as dangerous at 170 either. He has one ko at 170 in the ufc, being a clinch knee against an already chinny vet who isn’t in the ufc anymore. His other two he fought two awful strikers with no defense in McKee/Gooden, and he clinched both like his life depended on it. I am a big Chidi fan but I honestly think his finishing ability is a little overrated. The dude has some disgusting fundamentals and good power but he has been a flake his entire career. I remember hearing similar narratives about him when he fought Matthews, and everyone was loving him there only for him to get tapped inside a minute. Obv a different matchup but I don’t think Chidi is as dangerous as he’s made out to be here. He can’t fight the same way because all his energy goes towards making weight. We’ve only seen him absorb one really big shot at 170 (zds fight), and it put him on chicken legs. I’m not sure he can survive another war at 37 years old

Osbourne vs. Idiris
